definition: A woody, generally multi-stemmed plant that loses its leaves and becomes dormant during the winter. WSU

A tree that sheds its leaves at the end of a growing season and regrows them at the beginning of the next growing season. Most deciduous trees bear flowers and have woody stems and broad, rather than needle-like, leaves. WSU
Deer will eat any plant if hungry enough but if general forage options are favorable, they usually don't eat the plant identified as "deer-resistant".
A period of relatively long duration with substantially below-normal precipitation, usually occurring over a large area (WSU)

Drought tolerance is the ability to which a plant maintains its biomass production during arid or drought conditions. (Wikipedia)\n "Generally speaking, there are three major strategies employed by plants to survive annual drought conditions: desiccation tolerance; drought avoidance; and drought tolerance." https://ucanr.edu/blogs/blogcore/postdetail.cfm?postnum=28129
A woody, multi-stemmed shrub that retains its leaves throughout the year. WSU

Flammability refers to the ability of a plant to ignite and transfer heat and/or flames to surrounding plants or structures. Plants are flammable for different reasons; some plants are highly ignitable, but burn quickly. Other plants are not easily ignitable, but can burn for a long time once ignited. Flammability is comprised of four components:
Ignitability: The time to ignition once exposed to an ignition source such as an ember or flame.
Sustainability: The duration a plant will sustain a fire.
Combustibility: The rate of spread and rate of heat (or energy) given off from a burning plant.
Consumability: The percent of a plant consumed in a fire. (https://edis.ifas.ufl.edu/publication/fr151)

Plants that are "flammable" generally have several of these characteristics: contain fine, dry, or dead material within the plants, such as twigs, needles, and leaves; leaves, twigs, and stems contain volatile waxes, terpenes, or oils; leaves are aromatic (strong odor when crushed). OSU 2006  The flammability of a plant is reduced if it contains a lot of salt, soap, latex, and/or pectins. (Idaho Firewise)

Fire-reluctant plants are those that are slow to ignite when exposed to a heat source and once ignited are slow to propagate fire spread. A "fire resistant plant" can be defined the same, but in fire ecology, fire resistance refers to a plant that is adapted to fire by protective features such as thick bark or has the ability to reestablish after fire.\nFire-reluctant and fire resistant plants usually have high moisture content, high salt and saponin content, and low volatile oil and resin content. Other characteristics that contribute to flammability are age, size, total volume, and amount of dead material retained within and under the plant.

(1) High fire resistance means decreased flammability; (2) a species with….less flammable foliage that has a relatively lower probability of being killed or scarred by a fire than a fire sensitive [plant]. (National Wildfire Coordinating Group). (3) so resistant to fire that for a specified time and under conditions of a standard heat intensity it will not fail structurally or allow transit of heat and will not permit the side away from the fire to become hotter than a specified temperature. (Merriam Webster)\n"Fire-resistant plants are those that do not readily ignite from a flame or other ignition sources.  ...they tend to produce fewer embers, the ost common causefor homes burning in wildfires.  Their foliage and stems do not significantly contribute to fuel and fire intensity." (OSU, October 2023).
See "fire-reluctant" and "fire-resistant" plant.These plants are slower to ignite when exposed to heat and usually do not burn quickly. When they burn, they release energy more slowly.

A low-growing or trailing group of plants usually used to cover bare earth and create a uniform appearance. (WSU)   Groundcover provides protection of the topsoil from erosion and drought. In an ecosystem, the ground cover forms the layer of vegetation below the shrub layer known as the herbaceous layer. Wikipedia

A hardiness zone is a geographic area defined to encompass a certain range of climatic conditions relevant to plant growth and survival. The original and most widely used system was developed by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) as a rough guide for landscaping and gardening. The hardiness zones for the Rogue Valley are 7a,b and 8 a,b as evident on the USDA Plant Hardiness Map for Oregon https://www.gardeningknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2004/10/oregon_map+_lg.gif .
For a more thorough discussion on the hardiness zones attributed in this spreadsheet go to page 5 the OSU Fire-resistant Plants for Home Landscapes (ref 2). 
According to OSU's 2007 Garden Guide for the Rogue Valley, "The most suitable choices are "plants that need dry, hot summers and moist, but only moderately cold winters."
https://www.grit.com/farm-and-garden/understanding-zones/ for link to zone map
https://www.grit.com/farm-and-garden/understanding-zones/

Characteristics of "Highly Flammable Plants":
-- have low moisture content during fire season.  The moisture content of plants changes by time of day and year, climate and weather, maintenace regime, and more.  Because of a high variability, moisture content alone is not the best indicator of ignition potential. 
-- Plants have waxy or oily leaves or fragrant foliage because of chemical content. 
-- Plant structure is dense or retains dead materials, particularly fine debris.
-- Plants sheds a lot of bark, needles, leaves, or branches that accumlates on, within, or under the plant.
-- Plants have invasive qualities or climb/sprawl creating either horizontally or vertically continuous fuels.
-- Plants are prone to disease causing portions or all of the plant to die.
The area that extends from the home or other structure out 5 ft. No plants or other combustibles should occupy this area. .
The area that extends from 5-10 ft from the home and other flammable structures.  Only fire-resistent plants should be planted in this zone.

The area that extends from 10-30 ft from the home and other flammable structures. Low flammability plants should dominate this zone but some moderately flammable plants may be planted in the area if they are isolated and "properly maintained."
Extends from 30-100 ft from the home. The density and arrangement of zone 2 fire-resistant plants needs to be controlled to break-up horizontal and vertical continuity.
Extends from 50-100ft from the home. Idano Zone 3 rated plants may need to be pruned and thinning regularly to achieve and maintain fire-resistant plant characteristics.
A plant that is able to establish on many sites, grow quickly, and spread to the point of disrupting plant communities or ecosystems.
An invasive species of a plant which is designated as harmful to agricultural crops, ecosystems, or humans or livestock. WSU
An Oregon "native plant" grows in the wild without cultivation in the State of Oregon.  A "naturalized plant" is not considered "native" in this context.

A plant with soft, rather than woody, tissues. Perennials die back to the ground each year but their roots remain alive and send up new top growth each spring. (WSU) A perennial plant or simply perennial is a plant that lives more than two years. The term is often used to differentiate a plant from shorter-lived annuals and biennials. The term is also widely used to distinguish plants with little or no woody growth from trees and shrubs, which are also technically perennials. Wikipedia

In addition to proper siting of plants, it is important to properly maintain landscaping plants by: 
--removing dead vegetation, such as fallen leaves and needles, dead branches, dried grass and dry flowers, prior to and throughout fire season.
--irrigating sufficiently to maintain high plant moisture content during fire season.
--removing low tree branches to a height of at least 10 feet. Also remove small trees and shrubs from under the tree canopy. This will help prevent a surface fire from reaching the tree canopy. Do not remove more than a third of the total tree branches. (UNE)

Trees and shrubs that serve a screening function and are considered fire-resistant  do  not  readily  ignite  from  an  ember  or  other  ignition sources. These plants can be damaged or even killed by fire; however, their foliage and stems  do  not  significantly  contribute  to  the  fuel  load  and  therefore,  the  fire’s  intensity (Simes, 2012).

A woody perennial plant differing from a perennial herb by its persistent and woody stem, and from a tree by its low stature and habit of branching from the base. (WSU) A shrub is a small- to medium-sized perennial woody plant. Unlike herbaceous plants, shrubs have persistent woody stems above the ground. Shrubs can be deciduous or evergreen. They are distinguished from trees by their multiple stems and shorter height, less than 6–10 m tall. Wikipedia

A woody perennial plant, typically having a single stem or trunk growing to a considerable height and bearing lateral branches at some distance from the ground. (WSU)  In botany, a tree is a perennial plant with an elongated stem, or trunk, supporting branches and leaves in most species. In some usages, the definition of a tree may be narrower, including only wood plants with secondary growth, plants that are usable as lumber or plants above a specified height. Wikipedia

A plant having a long, slender stem that trails or creeps on the ground or climbs by winding itself about a support or holding fast with tendrils or claspers. (UW)  A vine is any plant with a growth habit of trailing or scandent stems, lianas or runners. The word vine can also refer to such stems or runners themselves, for instance, when used in wicker work. Wikipedia

Water need may be assumed to mean the relative amount of water a plant needs to survive and reproduce. The amount of water varies seasonally.  In this database, water need is assigned a relative value on a scale from high to very low. Ascription of water need is made by the sources listed.  In general, the sources that identified water need used a graphic such as a bucket with differing water levels or words like high, moderate, moderately-low, low, and very low.
The plant has properties or features that support wildlife generally, or birds, bats, and pollinators specifically as a host* or food source.

0-5 feet if the structure has one-hour flame-resistant siding OR 0-10 feet if the structure has non-flame-resistant siding. In this zone, use plants and materials with very low ignition potential. Plant no trees of shrubs and use only inorganic mulch.
5-30 feet from the house or structure
30 -100 feet from the house or structure
